questions.a: Basic unit in a neural network that receives input, applies weights and an activation function, and produces output.
Weights determine importance of inputs; biases shift the activation function for better fitting.
Functions that introduce non-linearity into the model, allowing networks to learn complex patterns.
Activation function that maps input to a value between 0 and 1; useful for binary classification.
Rectified Linear Unit - returns 0 if input is negative, otherwise returns input. Efficient and popular.
Turns raw scores into probabilities that sum to 1; used in the final layer for multiclass classification.
Input layer receives data; hidden layers learn features; output layer produces final prediction.
Forward pass calculates outputs; backpropagation updates weights based on error gradients.
Quantifies difference between predicted and true values; guides learning.
Epoch = full pass over data; batch = whole dataset; mini-batch = small subset used per update.
Neural network where connections don't form cycles; data moves one way, input to output.
NN type for image and spatial data; uses convolution layers to extract local features.
NN for sequential data; maintains state across time steps.
Special RNN that can learn long-term dependencies using memory cells.
Attention-based architecture for sequences; highly parallelizable and powerful for NLP tasks.
NN that learns to compress and then reconstruct input data; used for dimensionality reduction.
Controls the size of weight updates during training; too high = unstable, too low = slow.
Halts training when performance on validation set stops improving, to avoid overfitting.
How weights are set before training; impacts convergence and learning speed.
Data without target labels; used in unsupervised learning to find patterns or structure.
Grouping similar data points together without predefined labels.
Reducing number of input features while preserving important structure or variance.
Data points that differ significantly from others; can skew models or indicate anomalies.
Clustering algorithm that partitions data into k groups by minimizing within-cluster variance.
Builds a tree of clusters by progressively merging or splitting them based on distance.
Rules for computing distance between clusters (single, complete, average, ward).
Distance = shortest distance between any two points from two clusters.
Distance = longest distance between any two points from two clusters.
Distance = average distance between all pairs from two clusters.
Merges clusters to minimize increase in total within-cluster variance.
Reduces dimensionality by projecting data onto directions of maximum variance.
Learns compressed representations of input data without labels, then reconstructs it.
Autoencoder trained to reconstruct original input from noisy versions; improves robustness.
Autoencoder with constraint that forces many hidden units to be inactive; learns distinct features.
